events.text: [The figure doesn't look up. He's been here since before the grey came in — you can tell by the way the fog bends around him, like it learned to respect the shape he makes.]
You're in pain.
[Not a question.]
You think ending it — all of it — is mercy. That if nothing can feel, nothing can suffer.

choices.text: I think it's the only honest conclusion.

events.label: choice_contempt
events.text: Then you've decided suffering is the enemy. I wonder if that makes you afraid of it.
[He finally looks at you. His eyes are very calm. It's annoying.]
Come back when you've sat with it long enough to ask it a question.

events.label: choice_engage
events.text: Honest. I'll give you that. But conclusions from pain aren't conclusions — they're symptoms.
[You don't say anything. Neither does he, for a moment.]
I've suffered. More than I'll tell you. I chose to carry it rather than refuse it. Not because I enjoy the weight. Because refusing it made me smaller.

choices.text: Carrying it doesn't make it right.
I think I understand that. Even if I don't agree.

events.label: engage_pushback
events.text: No. But neither does eliminating the one who carries it. You'd take the stone from every hand in the world and call that freedom.
[He picks up a stone from the ground and holds it out. You take it without knowing why. It's heavier than it should be.]

events.label: engage_compassion
events.text: That's more than most give me. They usually either agree too quickly or argue too loudly.
Hold that distinction carefully. It's the difference between wisdom and surrender.

events.label: choice_listen
events.text: I think pain is the only honest teacher. Every shortcut around it leaves you softer in the places that matter.
[You watch him. He's not performing. That's what's unsettling.]

choices.text: That sounds like rationalising endless misery.
You actually believe that. You're not faking it.

events.label: listen_challenge
events.text: Maybe. Or maybe misery without meaning is just chemistry, and I chose to metabolise it differently.
I won't convince you. I just want you to see that I'm not afraid of the same thing you are.
No. I'm not. And you're the first person to say that plainly instead of trying to break it.
[Something shifts in the air. Very slightly. Like a window opening in a house that's been sealed for years.]
